Functionvoid std::__introsort_loop<__gnu_cxx::__normal_iterator > >, long, __gnu_cxx::__ops::_Iter_comp_iter >(__gnu_cxx::__normal_iterator > >, __gnu_cxx::__normal_iterator > >, long, __gnu_cxx::__ops::_Iter_comp_iter) [clone .isra.0]

          
Source file and linesstl_iterator.h:1111-1125

        
Modulecg.C

        

The loop is defined in:
  • /home/platy/Desktop/NPB-CG-CPP/NPB-OMP/CG/cg.cpp:848
  • /usr/include/c++/13/bits/move.h:197-199
  • /usr/include/c++/13/bits/stl_iterator.h:1111-1112,1125
  • /usr/include/c++/13/bits/stl_algo.h:1877-1882


Warnings:
Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
This loop has 4 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = (x<0 ? 0 : x) (or MAX(0,x) after defining the corresponding macro)

    • 
    

  

  

  


Vectorization


Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 26% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 3.96 to 1.00 cycles (3.96x speedup).

Details


25% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 25% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 33% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)



  

No data for this section

  


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • JMP: 0 occurrences


Slow data structures access


Detected data structures (typically arrays) that cannot be efficiently read/written

Details


  • Constant unknown stride: 1 occurrence(s)
Non-unit stride (uncontiguous) accesses are not efficiently using data caches


Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Type of elements and instruction set


No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This loop is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop


The binary loop does not contain any FP arithmetical operations.
The binary loop is loading 32 bytes.
The binary loop is storing 32 bytes.


  


General properties

nb uops23.75
loop length83.50
used x86 registers6
used mmx registers0
used xmm registers4
used ymm registers0
used zmm registers0
nb stack references0


Front-end


MACRO FUSION NOT POSSIBLE

micro-operation queue3.96 cycles
front end3.96 cycles


Back-end


      ALU0/BRU0ALU1ALU2ALU3/BRU1AGU0AGU1AGU2FP0FP1FP2FP3
uops1.941.941.941.942.171.832.000.001.003.000.00
cycles1.941.941.941.942.171.832.000.001.003.000.00

Execution ports to units layout:

  • ALU0/BRU0: ALU, BRU
  • ALU1: ALU
  • ALU2: ALU
  • ALU3/BRU1: ALU, BRU
  • AGU0 (256 bits): store address, load
  • AGU1 (256 bits): store address, load
  • AGU2: store address
  • FP0 (256 bits): VPU
  • FP1 (256 bits): VPU
  • FP2 (256 bits): VPU, FP store data
  • FP3 (256 bits): VPU, DIV/SQRT


Cycles executing div or sqrt instructionsNA
Longest recurrence chain latency (RecMII)1.00

Data deps.1.00

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 3.96 cycles. At this rate:

  • 12% of peak load performance is reached (8.24 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 25% of peak store performance is reached (8.24 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 3.50 to 3.00 cycles (1.17x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 26% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 3.50 to 1.00 cycles (3.50x speedup).

Details


25% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 25% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 33% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • JMP: 1 occurrences►

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays

No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This loop is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 3.50 cycles. At this rate:

  • 14% of peak load performance is reached (9.14 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 28% of peak store performance is reached (9.14 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 3.50 to 3.00 cycles (1.17x speedup).

Warnings:
Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
This loop has 4 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = (x<0 ? 0 : x) (or MAX(0,x) after defining the corresponding macro)

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 3.33 to 3.00 cycles (1.11x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 26% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 3.33 to 1.00 cycles (3.33x speedup).

Details


25% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 25% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 33% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

Warnings:
Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
This loop has 4 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = (x<0 ? 0 : x) (or MAX(0,x) after defining the corresponding macro)

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.50 to 3.00 cycles (1.50x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 26% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.50 to 1.00 cycles (4.50x speedup).

Details


25% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 25% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 33% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • JMP: 2 occurrences►

    • /usr/include/c++/13/bits/stl_iterator.h:1112
    • /usr/include/c++/13/bits/stl_algo.h:1877


Slow data structures access


Detected data structures (typically arrays) that cannot be efficiently read/written

Details


  • Constant unknown stride: 2 occurrence(s)
Non-unit stride (uncontiguous) accesses are not efficiently using data caches


Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Type of elements and instruction set


No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This loop is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop


The binary loop does not contain any FP arithmetical operations.
The binary loop is loading 32 bytes.
The binary loop is storing 32 bytes.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 4.50 cycles. At this rate:

  • 11% of peak load performance is reached (7.11 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 22% of peak store performance is reached (7.11 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.50 to 3.00 cycles (1.50x speedup).
